title: Preparing and loading a dip-pen nib
subtitle: Removing the factory coating, loading ink, and maintaining a broad-edge or pointed nib
excerpt: New dip-pen nibs are coated with a rust-preventing lacquer that repels ink until removed. This tutorial covers degreasing a new nib, loading ink correctly, cleaning between sessions, and reading the signs that a nib needs replacing.
sourceNotes: Nib preparation and maintenance methods are workshop practice, not documented in a single pre-1928 source. This synthesises the standard studio method common across broad-edge and pointed-pen traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A removable steel or bronze nib fitted into a pen holder. Dipped in ink for each writing session rather than using a cartridge or reservoir.
The two prongs of a dip nib. On a pointed nib, the tines flex apart under pressure to produce thick downstrokes and close together on the upstroke for hairlines.

content.text: Every new
dip nib

leaves the factory with a thin coating of oil or lacquer applied to prevent rust during storage and shipping. Until that coating is removed, the nib will repel ink rather than hold it. The first thing to do with any new nib is degrease it.
What you need

items.name: New dip nib (broad-edge Mitchell Roundhand or pointed Nikko G)
Pen holder (straight or oblique to match your hand and nib type)
Ink (iron-gall or sumi for practice; water-soluble for easy cleaning)
items.qty: Small amount in a shallow dish

items.name: Soft absorbent cloth or paper towel for drying

content.text: Degreasing a new nib

The simplest method: hold the nib in a lit match or lighter flame for 2 to 3 seconds. The heat burns off the lacquer. Allow to cool completely before touching or fitting to the holder. Check: dip in water and watch whether the water sheets off or beads. If it sheets, the nib is ready. If it still beads, repeat.

The cold method: wipe the nib with a cloth dampened with a small amount of distilled vinegar, then rinse in clean water and dry immediately with a cloth. This removes the lacquer by mild acid action. Suitable for nibs that cannot tolerate heat (some coated nibs).

Some calligraphers use their own saliva: the enzymes in saliva degrease a nib on contact. Press the nib between the lips briefly and it is ready to ink. This works but is less reliable than the heat method for heavily coated nibs.
Fitting the nib

Push the nib into the holder's collar from the side (not straight in). For a Mitchell Roundhand broad-edge nib: the small metal reservoir that sits under the nib should be positioned so it rests against the flat (inner) face of the nib, with the curved tip just touching the nib approximately 2 mm back from the writing edge. The reservoir holds the ink between strokes.
The nib should be snug in the collar, not loose or rattling. If it twists under writing pressure, adjust the collar grips with your thumbnail.
Loading ink
Dip the nib up to and including the reservoir, but not so far that ink enters the collar of the pen holder. Too much ink causes flooding; too little causes skipping.
Touch the tip of the nib lightly against the side of the ink dish or a scrap of paper to remove any excess drop hanging from the nib. One hanging droplet will flood the first stroke.
Write a test stroke on scrap paper. A correctly loaded nib produces a smooth stroke without skipping or flooding. The ink in the reservoir should last 3 to 5 strokes before re-loading.
Reading
tine
wear and replacing nibs

A broad-edge nib needs replacing when: the square writing edge develops a visible notch or rounded tip that catches the paper rather than gliding; ink no longer flows smoothly even after re-loading; or the reservoir clips out of alignment repeatedly. Pointed nibs need replacing when the tines spring apart at the base rather than staying parallel, or when the writing point scratches rather than slides. Both conditions usually appear after a period of heavy use measured in weeks or months of regular practice.
Cleaning and storage

At the end of every session, remove the nib from the holder and rinse it in clean water until the water runs clear. Dry it immediately and thoroughly with a cloth. Store nibs loose in a small box or stuck lightly into a cork; do not store wet. Iron-gall ink in particular corrodes a nib left wet overnight.
